topic.what: ACG clinical guideline for prevention of NSAID-related ulcer complications, including risk stratification and evidence-based prevention strategies
topic.how: Systematic evidence review and expert consensus establishing a risk-stratification framework (low/moderate/high GI risk) cross-referenced with cardiovascular risk, guiding choice among COX-2 selective inhibitors, PPI co-therapy, and misoprostol
topic.why_matters: NSAIDs are among the most widely used medications globally; this guideline provides the standard-of-care framework for balancing GI protection against cardiovascular safety, directly applicable to millions of patients on chronic NSAID therapy

characteristics.what_it_measures: Categorization of patients by GI and CV risk to guide NSAID prescribing and gastroprotection
characteristics.value: Three-tier GI risk (low/moderate/high) crossed with CV risk status; each combination maps to a specific prescribing recommendation
characteristics.vs_baseline: Formalized previously informal clinical judgment into a structured decision algorithm
characteristics.context: ACG practice guideline based on systematic review; widely adopted in US gastroenterology practice

good_for: Clinical decision-making for patients requiring chronic NSAID therapy
Risk stratification for GI complications in NSAID users
Balancing GI protection with cardiovascular safety in NSAID selection
Guideline-based PPI co-prescription and COX-2 inhibitor use
not_for: Non-NSAID causes of peptic ulcer disease
requires: Assessment of individual GI risk factors (prior ulcer, age, concomitant anticoagulant/corticosteroid/antiplatelet use)
H. pylori testing in patients with ulcer history starting NSAIDs

novelty: Introduced a dual-axis (GI risk x CV risk) decision matrix for NSAID prescribing that became the clinical standard
Explicitly addressed the post-rofecoxib landscape, integrating cardiovascular safety concerns into gastroprotection guidelines
Codified the role of PPI co-therapy as equivalent or superior to COX-2-selective inhibitors for GI protection in many risk strata
open_problems: Optimal strategy for patients at both high GI and high CV risk remains unsatisfactory (avoid NSAIDs if possible)
Long-term PPI co-therapy carries its own risks (fractures, C. difficile, hypomagnesemia) not fully addressed
Evidence gaps for newer anticoagulants (DOACs) in combination with NSAIDs
Guideline does not address topical or alternative analgesic strategies in detail
